coredata.dc:title: Exonuclease III-assisted CRISPR/Cas12a electrochemiluminescence biosensor for sub-femtomolar mercury ions determination
coredata.dc:creator: Hang, Xiao-Min
Zhao, Kai-Ren
Wang, Hui-Yi
Liu, Peng-Fei
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: A novel ECL biosensor was developed for ultrasensitive mercury ions (Hg2+) determination based on gold nanoparticles-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Au@CNDs) as luminophores and exonuclease III (Exo III) as well as CRISPR/Cas12a as signal amplifiers. The biosensor was fabricated by pre-modifying Au@CNDs hybrids and ferrocene-labeled single-stranded DNA (Fc-ssDNA) layer in sequence on the interface of a gold working electrode. The initial Au@CNDs ECL of the biosensor is quenched by Fc. Then, a thymine-rich ssDNA probe was designed for the recognition of Hg2+. In the presence of Hg2+, this thymine-rich ssDNA probe was folded to a DNA duplex based on T-Hg2+-T mismatches. Then, Exo III catalyzes the digestion of duplex of hairpin DNA, liberating short single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) product and releasing Hg2+. The produced ssDNA can be recognized by CRISPR/Cas12a and further trigger the collateral cleavage ability of CRISPR system, which can indiscriminately cut short Fc-ssDNA on the electrode, producing an ECL signal of Au@CNDs. Under the optimized conditions, a detection limit of 0.45 fM was realized for Hg2+ determination. The efficiency and robustness of this biosensor were further verified by using environmental water and human serum samples, which demonstrated its potential application in the environment and biological science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cas12a
Electrochemiluminescence
Exonuclease III
Collateral cleavage ability
Mercury ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CNDs) and Au@CNDs
The mechanism of ECL biosensor for Hg 2+ determination
Feasibility of ECL biosensor for Hg 2+ determination
Characterization of the fabrication process of biosensor
Selectivity, repeatability and stability of the ECL biosensor

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053 ) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. SJCX22_1873 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) PAGE analysis of various samples. lane 1: hairpin DNA; lane 2: hairpin DNA + Hg2+; lane 3: hairpin DNA + Exo III; lane 4: hairpin DNA + Hg2+ + Exo III; (B) ECL response of various samples: (hairpin DNA) + Cas12a-crRNA duplex (a), (hairpin DNA + Hg2+) + Cas12a-crRNA duplex (b), (hairpin DNA + Exo III) + Cas12a-crRNA duplex (c), RNase-free water + Cas12a-crRNA duplex (d), and (hairpin DNA + Hg2+ + Exo III) + Cas12a-crRNA duplex (e). Concentration of every component used in PAGE, hairpin DNA: 1 μM, Hg2+: 1 nM, Exo III: 30 U, Cas12a-crRNA duplex: 50 nM.
CV and EIS characterization of the fabrication process of biosensor. (a) bare GE, (b) Au@CNDs/GE, (c) Fc-ssDNA/Au@CNDs/GE and (d) MCH/Fc-ssDNA/Au@CNDs/GE.

(A) Optimization of significant experimental conditions including concentration of hairpin DNA, (B) concentration of Exo III, (C) enzymatic degradation time and (D) cleavage time of Cas12a. The concentration of Hg2+ was 1 nM throughout the optimization process. (Error bars: SD, n = 3).

(A) ECL response of different concentration of Hg2+ (0, 10 fM, 100 fM, 1 pM, 10 pM, 100 pM and 1 nM. (B) Calibration curve between ECL intensity (I) and logarithm of concentration of Hg2+ (lgC). (Error bars: SD, n = 3).

The selectivity (A) and repeatability (B) analysis of the ECL biosensor. Concentrations of interferences (Ag+, Na+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+): 1 nM, concentration of Hg2+: 300 pM. (Error bars: SD, n = 3).
Illustration of ECL biosensor for Hg2+ determination based on Exo III-mediated enzymatical degradation and Cas12a.
ECL biosensor for Hg2+ determination in real samples.

head.ce:title: Exonuclease III-assisted CRISPR/Cas12a electrochemiluminescence biosensor for sub-femtomolar mercury ions determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ECL biosensor was developed for ultrasensitive mercury ions (Hg2+) determination based on gold nanoparticles-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Au@CNDs) as luminophores and exonuclease III (Exo III) as well as CRISPR/Cas12a as signal amplifiers. The biosensor was fabricated by pre-modifying Au@CNDs hybrids and ferrocene-labeled single-stranded DNA (Fc-ssDNA) layer in sequence on the interface of a gold working electrode. The initial Au@CNDs ECL of the biosensor is quenched by Fc. Then, a thymine-rich ssDNA probe was designed for the recognition of Hg2+. In the presence of Hg2+, this thymine-rich ssDNA probe was folded to a DNA duplex based on T-Hg2+-T mismatches. Then, Exo III catalyzes the digestion of duplex of hairpin DNA, liberating short single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) product and releasing Hg2+. The produced ssDNA can be recognized by CRISPR/Cas12a and further trigger the collateral cleavage ability of CRISPR system, which can indiscriminately cut short Fc-ssDNA on the electrode, producing an ECL signal of Au@CNDs. Under the optimized conditions, a detection limit of 0.45 fM was realized for Hg2+ determination. The efficiency and robustness of this biosensor were further verified by using environmental water and human serum samples, which demonstrated its potential application in the environment and biological science.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most toxic metals and is found globally in the natural environment [1–3]. It can be enriched in the human body through environmental media, causing irreversible damage to the central nervous system, kidneys, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal tract, and immune system, and is a serious threat to human health even at low concentrations [4–6]. Therefore, it is necessary to monitor and detect trace Hg2+ in environments. Apart from environmental monitoring, a study of the Hg2+ uptake, distribution, and excretion in vivo may aid people to understand the mechanism underlying some physiological and pathological events and develop new strategies for avoiding and/or minimizing the toxic effects induced by Hg2+ and its compounds [7]. Therefore, the development of highly sensitive methods to detect trace amounts of Hg2+ both in the environment and in vivo system is of great importance.

The most widely used techniques for Hg2+ determination are atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [8], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [9], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) methods [10], they yet require large instrument, complex sample pre-treatment and trained personnel, which limit their availability in low infrastructure laboratories with limited resources available. Although several simple methods (such as colorimetric and fluorescent) have been developed, they, however, inherently lack selectivity in a complex matrix [11–13]. The recent discovery that Hg2+ can specifically induce T-Hg2+-T mismatches in DNA duplexes, which has been developed for a series of innovative Hg2+ sensing assays by combining with different approaches including fluorescent [14], colorimetric [15], electrochemical [16], and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [11,17] methods, presenting an excellent opportunity for accurate, sensitive and simple Hg2+ determination. Among these techniques, ECL has engaged great awareness due to its inherent superiorities such as near-zero background, wide dynamic range, high sensitivity, and simple instruments [18,19]. Despite great progress, fabrication of ECL biosensors with high sensitivity for Hg2+ determination remains a hurdle, due to the low luminescence efficiency of conventional ECL luminophors, such as luminol and ruthenium complexes [20,21].

Over the past decades, two strategies are mainly employed to improve the sensitivity of the ECL system. One is the utilization of high luminous efficiency advanced materials [20,22]. Based on their high specific surface area, excellent electrical conductivity and catalytic performance, various nanomaterials including metallic, metal oxides, quantum dots, carbon nanomaterials, and hybrid nanostructures have been applied in ECL sensors for remarkably amplifying the signals [23–25]. Recently, we showed that gold nanoparticles-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Au@CNDs) is a high-efficiency ECL luminophore and has been used for fabricating sensitive biosensors to detect cells and nucleic acids [26]. The other strategy is employing signal amplification, which is often utilized for nucleic acid-related target analysis [27,28]. In particular, the recent discovered collateral cleavage properties of some CRISPR/Cas proteins (i.e. Cas12a and Cas13a) have captured significant attention [29–33]. Typically, Cas12a and Cas13a nucleases can degrade nonspecific reporter oligos (collateral cleavage ability), once they recognize a specific DNA/RNA target, therefore can dramatically increase the sensitivity of detection [34–36]. As a result, they have demonstrated enormous potential for nucleic-acid-related detection in many applications [37–40]. However, few of them have been used for the detection of heavy metal ions [41,42].

Here, we report an ECL biosensor based on Exo III degrade, Hg2+-mediated T-T base-pairing, CRISPR/Cas12a collateral cleavage, and high luminous efficiency luminophores of Au@CNDs for Hg2+ detection. As expected, this biosensor can detect Hg2+ with high sensitivity and high selectivity. The good analytical performance in environmental water and human serum samples indicates that the ECL biosensor might have great potential for application in the environment and biological science. This work also provides a new case of CRISPR/Cas systems for other toxic metal ions determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CNDs) and Au@CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CNDs and Au@CNDs were similar to our previous research and the detailed synthesis procedure was placed in the Supporting Material (SM) [26,37]. In addition, their morphological and size were exhibited in Fig. S1.

Prior to ECL measurement, the GE was cleaned by a physical polishing and an electrochemical scanning process in sequence [40]. Then, 10 μL diluted Au@CNDs was dripped onto the as-cleaned GE and incubated in a circumambient environment to form Au@CNDs/GE. Afterward, 10 μL Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP)-treated thiolated Fc-ssDNA (2 μM) was spread on the electrode surface and incubated at 4 ℃ for 16 h to generate Fc-ssDNA/Au@CNDs/GE. After a rinsing procedure, 5 μL MCH (1 mM) was cast on the interface of as-prepared electrode and incubated for another 40 min to take up nonspecific sites. Finally, the modified electrode was washed and placed at 4 ℃ for subsequent use.

A variety of concentrations of Hg2+ (5 μL) were first mixed with hairpin DNA (5 μL, 100 nM). 1 μL Exo III (30 U) was introduced into the mixture and incubated at 37 ℃ for 60 min to realize the enzymatic hydrolysis. Then, the solution was heated to 80 ℃ for 10 min to inactivate the Exo III activity. Thereafter, 10 μL Cas12a-crRNA duplexes (50 nM) were involved and further incubated for 10 min to trigger the collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a. Subsequently, the above solution was present on the as-prepared electrode and allowed to react for 40 min at 37 ℃. Ultimately, the electrode was rinsed with PBS and transferred into the ECL testing solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of ECL biosensor for Hg 2+ determination
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL biosensor for Hg2+ determination is illustrated in Scheme 1. Initially, the as-synthesized Au@CNDs was immobilized on the electrode interface to get an original signal. And Fc-ssDNA as a stochastic ssDNA was modified on the interface of Au@CNDs to quench its ECL emission. For Hg2+ detection, the hairpin DNA was elaborated which consists of three domains: two protruding domains for Hg2+ recognition (a, a*), a stem domain for the generation of meta-stable state in the absence of Hg2+ (b-b*), and a loop domain (c). When the Hg2+ was present, the domain a and a* formed a stable duplex structure with blunt terminus by virtue of Hg2+-mediated T-T base-pairing. Then, Exo III could progressively digest domain a* and domain b* at 3'-termini of hairpin DNA, leading to the reutilization of Hg2+. After the progressive degradation and N cycles, plenty of fragments containing activator (domain bc) were generated, in which the activator could hybridize with crRNA to form a Cas12a-crRNA-activator ternary complex and further activate the collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a. Since the solution containing the Cas12a-crRNA-activator ternary complex was present on the modified electrode, the Fc-ssDNA could be indiscriminately cleaved because of the prominent collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a, thus recovering ECL emission of Au@CNDs. However, in the absence of Hg2+, the protruding domains in the hairpin DNA could resist the degradation of Exo III and remain intact. The collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a, thus, could not be activated and the Fc-ssDNA remained intact, as well as no recovery of ECL emission of Au@CNDs. As a result, the Hg2+ could be quantified via the recovery of ECL emission of Au@CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of ECL biosensor for Hg 2+ determination
ce:section.ce:para: The process of Exo III-mediated enzymatical degradation was investigated by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) and the results were depicted in Fig. 1 A, lane 1 and lane 2 were the bands of hairpin DNA without and with the addition of Hg2+. The hairpin DNA remained intact in the presence of Exo III and the absence of Hg2+ (lane 3), which is attributed to that the protruding domains a and a* could resist the degradation of Exo III. However, when the hairpin DNA, Hg2+ and Exo III coexisted, a new band, which performed faster mobility than the hairpin DNA (lane 1), could be observed in lane 4. Meanwhile, the band of hairpin DNA disappeared. The reason for this phenomenon is the effective degradation of Exo III on the duplex structure with blunt terminus triggered by Hg2+-mediated T-T base-pairing. These results demonstrated the generation of the duplex structure with blunt terminus triggered by Hg2+-mediated T-T base-pairing, the generation of fragments by Exo III degradation, and the activation of collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a. Subsequently, the Cas12a-crRNA-activator ternary complex binding and collateral cleavage ability were verified. The results were shown in Fig. S2.

Furthermore, ECL was employed to assess the feasibility of the ECL biosensor. For this purpose, various samples were utilized including hairpin DNA, hairpin DNA with the addition of Hg2+, hairpin DNA with the addition of Exo III, RNase-free water, and hairpin DNA with the addition of Hg2+ and Exo III. After enzymatic hydrolysis and inactivating the Exo III activity, Cas12a-crRNA duplex was introduced into the five samples, respectively. Later, the mixtures were dripped onto the fabricated biosensing electrodes. After reaction, the electrode was transferred into the ECL testing solution for measurement, and the results were depicted in Fig. 1B. For the first four samples, negligible recoveries of ECL signal were observed (curves a, b, c, and d), while only the sample containing hairpin DNA with the addition of Hg2+ and Exo III induced significant recovery of ECL emission (curve e). The ECL results further testified the feasibility of the designed biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fabrication process of biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: CV was first recruited to validate the fabrication process of the biosensor. As depicted in Fig. 2A, the bare GE performed an initial CV peak (curve a). After modification with Au@CNDs, the peak current decreased slightly (curve b), the reason is that the modification of Au@CNDs prevented electron transfer between the electrode and [Fe(CN)6]3⁻/4⁻ in solution. When the Fc-ssDNA was immobilized on the surface of the electrode, a further decrease of peak current could be seen (curve c), which is possibly ascribed to that Fc-ssDNA inhibited the transmission of electrons. The current response further decreased after the modification of non-conductive MCH (curve d). Additionally, EIS was also employed to further examine the fabrication procedure (Fig. 2B), and the results were in great consistence with that of CV, demonstrating the successful fabrication process of the electrode.

To gain the optimal sensing performance, a series of experimental conditions including the concentrations of Au@CNDs, Fc-ssDNA, hairpin DNA, and Exo-III, Exo III-mediated enzymatic degradation time, and cleavage time of Cas12a, were studied. The concentration of Au@CNDs highly related to the biosensor performance was first assessed and the result was shown in Fig. S3A. The ECL intensity gradually enhanced with the increasing concentration of Au@CNDs, and reached a platform at 0.75 mg mL⁻1. Thus, the optimal concentration of Au@CNDs was fixed at 0.75 mg mL⁻1. Fc as a quencher can efficiently quench the ECL of Au@CNDs via energy transfer or the electron transfer mechanism. Therefore, the initial signal of the biosensor (off signal) is highly dependent on the concentration of Fc-ssDNA, which was then evaluated. As shown in Fig. S3B, the ECL signal gradually decreased with the concentration of Fc-ssDNA from 0.5 μM to 2 μM, and then reached a platform. Thus, the optimal concentration of Fc-ssDNA was fixed at 2 μM. The concentration of hairpin DNA was also examined due to the close association with the Exo III-mediated degradation process, further hybridization with the crRNA, and activation of collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a. As shown in Fig. 3A, the ECL intensity continually increased with the concentration of hairpin DNA ranging from 20 nM to 100 nM. Therefore, the concentration of hairpin DNA was determined at 100 nM. Next, the concentration of Exo III was estimated and appointed as 30 U (Fig. 3B). Afterward, we optimized the Exo III-mediated enzymatic degradation time. The results in Fig. 3C exhibited that the ECL signal reached the maximum when the time extended to 60 min, which was utilized as an appropriate enzymatic degradation time for Exo III. Subsequently, we investigated the collateral cleavage time of Cas12a (Fig. 3D) and determined 40 min as the optimal condition. Finally, the concentration of Cas12a-crRNA duplex was evaluated and 50 nM Cas12a-crRNA duplex was used as the optimal concentration for Fc-ssDNA cleavage (Fig. S4).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, we evaluated the quantification performance of the ECL biosensor. A variety of concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 10 fM, 100 fM, 1 pM, 10 pM, 100 pM and 1 nM) was involved. As shown in Fig. 4A, the ECL intensity gradually enhanced with the concentration of Hg2+ from 0 to 1 nM. And Fig. 4B showed that the ECL intensity was positively correlated to the logarithm of the concentration of Hg2+. The corresponding correlation equation was I = 1709 lgC + 25820 (R2 = 0.9965) with a detection limit of 0.45 fM (S/N = 3), which was superior to most reports for Hg2+ detection (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability and stability of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the ECL biosensor, various metal ions including Ag+, Na+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and their mixture containing Hg2+ were used, while the PBS was selected the blank control. All above samples were proceeded the whole detection process at the same experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5A, only the Hg2+ and mixture containing Hg2+ induced significant change in ECL signal, while all other interferences displayed a negligible impact on the detection system even in higher concentrations. The results presented that only Hg2+ could mediate T-T base-pairing, subsequent Exo III-mediated enzymatic degradation and further activate the collateral cleavage of Cas12a on stochastic Fc-ssDNA, demonstrating a desirable selectively of the proposed biosensor.

Furthermore, we probed the repeatability of the proposed biosensor by using five parallel experiments with the same concentrations of Hg2+. The results in Fig. 5B showed that ECL signals performed a tiny distinction with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.5%, indicating satisfactory repeatability of the ECL biosensor. Ultimately, we checked the stability of the biosensor. To this end, the biosensor was stored at 4 ℃ for different periods of time and was utilized to determine Hg2+. The ECL signal decreased only less than 5% after storing for 15 days (Fig. S5), suggesting the excellent stability of the proposed biosensor.

To validate whether the proposed biosensor could analyze Hg2+ with different concentrations of Hg2+ (20 fM, 50 pM, and 1 nM) in real samples, tap water, river, lake water, and human serum sample were recruited. The tap water, river water, lake water, and human serum sample were first spiked into different concentrations of Hg2+, subsequently preceded the detection procedure and the results could be observed in Table 1. The recoveries ranged from 95.0% to 104.0% and its RSD ranged from 2.6% to 4.8% in tap water. The recoveries ranged from 99.0% to 102.0% and its RSD ranged from 2.1% to 3.9% in river water. The recoveries ranged from 96.0% to 102.8% and its RSD ranged from 3.2% to 4.8% in lake water. The recoveries ranged from 96.7% to 101.5% and its RSD ranged from 4.5% to 5.3% in the human serum sample. The results declared that the ECL biosensor possessed eminent practicability.

In summary, we proposed an ECL biosensor integrating Exo III and CRISPR/Cas12a to achieve ultrasensitive determination of mercury ions. Benefited from Exo III-mediated enzymatic degradation and brilliant collateral cleavage capability of Cas12a, the change in the Hg2+concentration could be directly quantified through the ECL signal. The introduction of Exo III could reduce the cost of chemically modified nucleic acids. The proposed biosensor realized ultrasensitive Hg2+ determination at sub-femtomolar level (LOD, 0.45 fM) under Exo III and Cas12a-mediated dual amplification, which is superior to most reports for Hg2+ assay. In addition, the ECL biosensor exhibited so satisfactory selectivity and practicability that can effectively avoid possible background generated by other ions in water samples. Furthermore, the work offers a versatile platform for other toxic metal ions determination.

Xiao-Min Hang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Kai-Ren Zhao: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Hui-Yi Wang: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Peng-Fei Liu: Software, Validation, Visualization. Li Wang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. SJCX22_1873).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132208.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence sensor based on biosynthetic CdSe/CdS quantum dots and liposome carrier signal amplification for mercury detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiao-Min Hang is presently pursuing her master degree in biology and medicine in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Kai-Ren Zhao is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Hui-Yi Wang is presently pursuing her master degree in biology and medicine in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.
Peng-Fei Liu is presently pursuing his master degree in chemistry in Hunan Normal University. His current research focuses on biosensing and gene regulation.

Li Wang received her M. S. degree and Ph. D. degree from ShaanXi Normal University. She is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. Her current research interests include multifunctional nanomaterial and their applications in diagnosis, metastasis, and treatment of cancer.
